objective_text: Probability, Sample Spaces, and Set Foundations
skills_targeted: computing a conditional probability directly from two given counts
using the conditioning group as the denominator rather than the whole population
question_text: In a class of 30 students, 18 play a sport. Of the students who play a sport, 12 are also in band. Given that a student plays a sport, what is the probability that the student is in band?
explanation: The condition 'plays a sport' restricts attention to the 18 sport players. Of those, 12 are in band, so P(band given sport) = 12/18 = 2/3.

distractor_logic.A: Correct: among the 18 sport players, 12 are in band, so 12/18 = 2/3.
distractor_logic.B: Student divides by the whole class of 30 instead of the 18 sport players, getting 12/30 = 2/5.
distractor_logic.C: Student reports the probability of playing a sport (18/30 = 3/5) and ignores the band information.
distractor_logic.D: Student uses the 6 sport players who are not in band (18 - 12) as the numerator, getting 6/18 = 1/3.

strategy_hints.hint_text: The phrase 'given that a student plays a sport' tells you the denominator is the sport players, not the whole class.

author: jd-oviedo

objective_text: Probability, Sample Spaces, and Set Foundations
skills_targeted: computing a conditional probability from a described subgroup
identifying the restricted sample space named by the condition
question_text: A bag has 10 marbles: 6 are red and 4 are blue. Of the red marbles, 2 have a star printed on them. A marble is drawn and it is red. Given that it is red, what is the probability that it has a star?
explanation: Given the marble is red, only the 6 red marbles matter. Of those, 2 have a star, so P(star given red) = 2/6 = 1/3.

distractor_logic.A: Student divides by all 10 marbles instead of the 6 red ones, getting 2/10 = 1/5.
distractor_logic.B: Student reports the probability of drawing red (6/10 = 3/5) and ignores the star condition.
distractor_logic.C: Correct: among the 6 red marbles, 2 have a star, so 2/6 = 1/3.
distractor_logic.D: Student forms a ratio of star reds to non-star reds (2 to 4) instead of dividing by all 6 reds, getting 2/4 = 1/2.

strategy_hints.hint_text: 'Given that it is red' means your whole world shrinks to just the red marbles. Count stars among those.

author: jd-oviedo

objective_text: Probability, Sample Spaces, and Set Foundations
skills_targeted: computing a conditional probability from survey counts
restricting the denominator to the conditioning group
question_text: In a survey of 40 people, 25 own a dog. Of the dog owners, 15 also own a cat. Given that a person owns a dog, what is the probability that the person also owns a cat?
explanation: Given the person owns a dog, only the 25 dog owners matter. Of those, 15 own a cat, so P(cat given dog) = 15/25 = 3/5.

distractor_logic.A: Correct: among the 25 dog owners, 15 own a cat, so 15/25 = 3/5.
distractor_logic.B: Student divides by all 40 people instead of the 25 dog owners, getting 15/40 = 3/8.
distractor_logic.C: Student reports the probability of owning a dog (25/40 = 5/8) and ignores the cat condition.
distractor_logic.D: Student uses the 10 dog owners without a cat (25 - 15) as the numerator, getting 10/25 = 2/5.

strategy_hints.hint_text: The condition is 'owns a dog', so the denominator is the 25 dog owners, not all 40 people.

author: jd-oviedo

objective_text: Probability, Sample Spaces, and Set Foundations
skills_targeted: computing a conditional probability from a described card subgroup
using the conditioning count as the denominator
question_text: A set of 20 cards has 8 red cards. Of the red cards, 3 are marked with a star. A card is drawn and it is red. Given that it is red, what is the probability that it is marked with a star?
explanation: Given the card is red, only the 8 red cards matter. Of those, 3 are marked, so P(marked given red) = 3/8.

distractor_logic.A: Student divides by all 20 cards instead of the 8 red ones, getting 3/20.
distractor_logic.B: Student reports the probability of drawing red (8/20 = 2/5) and ignores the marked condition.
distractor_logic.C: Student forms a ratio of marked reds to unmarked reds (3 to 5) instead of dividing by all 8 reds, getting 3/5.
distractor_logic.D: Correct: among the 8 red cards, 3 are marked, so 3/8.

strategy_hints.hint_text: Once you know the card is red, ignore every non-red card. Count the marked cards among the 8 reds.

author: jd-oviedo

objective_text: Probability, Sample Spaces, and Set Foundations
skills_targeted: computing a conditional probability from a described membership scenario
restricting the sample space to the conditioning group
question_text: A club has 24 members. 15 of them are seniors. Of the seniors, 10 are officers. Given that a member is a senior, what is the probability that the member is an officer?
explanation: Given the member is a senior, only the 15 seniors matter. Of those, 10 are officers, so P(officer given senior) = 10/15 = 2/3.

distractor_logic.A: Student divides by all 24 members instead of the 15 seniors, getting 10/24 = 5/12.
distractor_logic.B: Correct: among the 15 seniors, 10 are officers, so 10/15 = 2/3.
distractor_logic.C: Student reports the probability of being a senior (15/24 = 5/8) and ignores the officer condition.
distractor_logic.D: Student uses the 5 seniors who are not officers (15 - 10) as the numerator, getting 5/15 = 1/3.

strategy_hints.hint_text: 'Given that a member is a senior' fixes the denominator at 15 seniors. Count officers within that group.

author: jd-oviedo

objective_text: Probability, Sample Spaces, and Set Foundations
skills_targeted: deriving a conditioning-group total from a two-way breakdown before computing a conditional probability
distinguishing the condition from the reversed condition
question_text: A group of students was surveyed: 12 like both soccer and basketball, 8 like soccer but not basketball, 6 like basketball but not soccer, and 4 like neither. Given that a student likes soccer, what is the probability that the student also likes basketball?
explanation: The soccer group is 12 + 8 = 20 students. Of those, 12 also like basketball, so P(basketball given soccer) = 12/20 = 3/5.

distractor_logic.A: Student uses the 6 basketball-only students as the numerator over the soccer group, getting 6/20 = 3/10.
distractor_logic.B: Correct: the soccer group is 12 + 8 = 20, of whom 12 like basketball, so 12/20 = 3/5.
distractor_logic.C: Student divides by all 30 students (12 + 8 + 6 + 4) instead of the 20 soccer fans, getting 12/30 = 2/5.
distractor_logic.D: Student conditions on basketball instead of soccer, using the basketball group of 12 + 6 = 18 to get 12/18 = 2/3.

strategy_hints.hint_text: First add up everyone who likes soccer, both the soccer-and-basketball group and the soccer-only group. That total is your denominator.
Conditioning on soccer is not the same as conditioning on basketball. Watch for the answer that swaps the two groups.

author: jd-oviedo

objective_text: Probability, Sample Spaces, and Set Foundations
skills_targeted: computing a conditional probability from a sequential without-replacement scenario
updating both the favorable count and the total after the conditioning event
question_text: A bag contains 5 red marbles and 3 green marbles. Two marbles are drawn without replacement. Given that the first marble drawn is red, what is the probability that the second marble is also red?
explanation: After a red marble is removed, 4 red marbles remain out of 7 total. So P(second red given first red) = 4/7.

distractor_logic.A: Correct: after one red is removed, 4 reds remain out of 7, so 4/7.
distractor_logic.B: Student ignores the condition and reports the probability of red on the first draw, 5/8.
distractor_logic.C: Student lowers the total to 7 but forgets to lower the red count, using 5/7.
distractor_logic.D: Student lowers the red count to 4 but forgets to lower the total, using 4/8 = 1/2.

strategy_hints.hint_text: The condition tells you a red marble is already gone. Lower both the red count and the total by one before computing the second draw.
A choice that changes only the count or only the total has updated just one of the two numbers that should change.

author: jd-oviedo

objective_text: Probability, Sample Spaces, and Set Foundations
skills_targeted: computing a conditional probability when the conditioning total is given but the joint count must be located
separating P(A given B) from P(B given A) in a two-way scenario
question_text: Of 40 people at an event, 22 are adults and 18 are children. In total, 30 people passed a quiz, and 16 of the adults passed. Given that a person is an adult, what is the probability that the person passed the quiz?
explanation: Given the person is an adult, the denominator is the 22 adults. Of those, 16 passed, so P(passed given adult) = 16/22 = 8/11.

distractor_logic.A: Student divides by all 40 people instead of the 22 adults, getting 16/40 = 2/5.
distractor_logic.B: Student reports the probability of being an adult (22/40 = 11/20) and ignores the quiz result.
distractor_logic.C: Correct: among the 22 adults, 16 passed, so 16/22 = 8/11.
distractor_logic.D: Student conditions on having passed instead of being an adult, using the 30 passers to get 16/30 = 8/15.

strategy_hints.hint_text: The condition is 'is an adult', so the denominator is the 22 adults, not the 30 who passed and not all 40 people.
Conditioning on 'adult' versus conditioning on 'passed' gives different denominators. Make sure you use the adult total.

author: jd-oviedo

objective_text: Probability, Sample Spaces, and Set Foundations
skills_targeted: computing a conditional probability as the ratio of a joint probability to the conditioning probability
dividing two percentages rather than subtracting or adding them
question_text: At a school, 60% of students take Spanish. Of all students, 24% take both Spanish and art. Given that a student takes Spanish, what is the probability that the student also takes art?
explanation: P(art given Spanish) = P(both) divided by P(Spanish) = 24% divided by 60% = 0.24 / 0.60 = 0.40, which is 40%.

distractor_logic.A: Student reports the joint probability of taking both (24%) without dividing by the Spanish probability.
distractor_logic.B: Student subtracts the two percentages (60% - 24% = 36%) instead of dividing.
distractor_logic.C: Student adds the two percentages (60% + 24% = 84%) instead of dividing.

strategy_hints.hint_text: A conditional probability is the joint probability divided by the probability of the condition. Divide the 'both' percent by the Spanish percent.

author: jd-oviedo

objective_text: Probability, Sample Spaces, and Set Foundations
skills_targeted: computing a conditional probability for a different-colored second draw without replacement
recognizing that a removed marble changes the total but not the count of the other color
question_text: A bag contains 4 red marbles and 6 blue marbles. Two marbles are drawn without replacement. Given that the first marble drawn is blue, what is the probability that the second marble is red?
explanation: Removing a blue marble leaves the 4 red marbles unchanged but lowers the total to 9. So P(second red given first blue) = 4/9.

distractor_logic.A: Student ignores the condition and uses the starting probability of red, 4/10 = 2/5.
distractor_logic.B: Correct: the red count stays at 4 while the total drops to 9, so 4/9.
distractor_logic.C: Student wrongly lowers the red count to 3, as if the first marble had been red, using 3/9 = 1/3.
distractor_logic.D: Student computes the probability that the second marble is blue (5 blue left out of 9), getting 5/9, instead of red.

strategy_hints.hint_text: The first marble was blue, so the red count is untouched. Only the total drops, from 10 to 9.
A choice that lowers the red count has confused a blue first draw with a red one. The reds are still all there.

author: jd-oviedo

objective_text: Probability, Sample Spaces, and Set Foundations
skills_targeted: computing a conditional probability that reverses the more obvious direction in a two-way scenario
deriving the conditioning total by combining two subgroup counts
question_text: Among 100 people, 40 are men and 60 are women. 30 of the men own a car and 24 of the women own a car. Given that a randomly chosen person owns a car, what is the probability that the person is a man?
explanation: The condition is 'owns a car'. The car owners total 30 + 24 = 54. Of those, 30 are men, so P(man given car owner) = 30/54 = 5/9.

distractor_logic.A: Student reports the probability of being a man overall (40/100 = 2/5), ignoring the car-owner condition.
distractor_logic.B: Student divides the men-with-cars by all 100 people, getting 30/100 = 3/10, instead of by the car owners.
distractor_logic.C: Correct: there are 54 car owners, of whom 30 are men, so 30/54 = 5/9.
distractor_logic.D: Student reverses the condition and computes the probability that a man owns a car (30/40 = 3/4) rather than the probability a car owner is a man.

strategy_hints.hint_text: The condition is 'owns a car', so first find the total number of car owners by adding the men and women who own one.
P(man given car owner) is not the same as P(car owner given man). Watch for the answer that uses the 40 men as the denominator.

author: jd-oviedo

objective_text: Probability, Sample Spaces, and Set Foundations
skills_targeted: using inclusion-exclusion to find the conditioning group before computing a conditional probability
interpreting 'given at least one' as conditioning on the union of two events
question_text: In a group of 60 students, 35 take math, 30 take science, and 15 take both. Given that a student takes at least one of the two subjects, what is the probability that the student takes both?
explanation: The number taking at least one subject is 35 + 30 - 15 = 50. Of those, 15 take both, so P(both given at least one) = 15/50 = 3/10.

distractor_logic.A: Student forgets to subtract the overlap and uses 35 + 30 = 65 as the 'at least one' total, getting 15/65 = 3/13.
distractor_logic.B: Correct: at least one is 35 + 30 - 15 = 50, of whom 15 take both, so 15/50 = 3/10.
distractor_logic.C: Student conditions on taking math only, using the 35 math students to get 15/35 = 3/7.
distractor_logic.D: Student divides by all 60 students instead of the 'at least one' group, getting 15/60 = 1/4.

strategy_hints.hint_text: 'At least one' is the union of math and science. Use inclusion-exclusion: add the two subjects and subtract the overlap counted twice.
Adding 35 and 30 without removing the 15 overlap double counts the both-subject students. Rule out answers built on 65.

author: jd-oviedo

objective_text: Probability, Sample Spaces, and Set Foundations
skills_targeted: computing a conditional compound probability over a restricted sample space
applying a condition that removes a whole category before two dependent draws
question_text: A bag contains 3 red, 4 blue, and 5 green marbles. Two marbles are drawn without replacement. Given that neither marble drawn is green, what is the probability that both marbles are red?
explanation: The condition 'neither is green' restricts the draws to the 7 non-green marbles (3 red and 4 blue). Within that group, P(both red) = 3/7 times 2/6 = 6/42 = 1/7.

distractor_logic.A: Correct: among the 7 non-green marbles, 3/7 times 2/6 = 6/42 = 1/7.
distractor_logic.B: Student ignores the condition and draws from all 12 marbles, using 3/12 times 2/11 = 6/132 = 1/22.
distractor_logic.C: Student uses the restricted group of 7 but treats the draws as with replacement, using 3/7 times 3/7 = 9/49.
distractor_logic.D: Student uses the restricted group of 7 but forgets the total drops for the second draw, using 3/7 times 2/7 = 6/49.

strategy_hints.hint_text: 'Neither is green' means you can set the green marbles aside entirely. Work only within the 7 non-green marbles.
A choice that draws from 12 marbles has ignored the condition. The denominator should start at 7, not 12.

author: jd-oviedo

objective_text: Probability, Sample Spaces, and Set Foundations
skills_targeted: computing a reversed conditional probability from a probability-tree scenario
building the conditioning total from two separate branch probabilities
question_text: At a school, half of all students study for a test. Of those who study, 60% pass. Of those who do not study, 20% pass. Given that a randomly chosen student passed, what is the probability that the student had studied?
explanation: P(study and pass) = 0.5 times 0.6 = 0.30. P(no study and pass) = 0.5 times 0.2 = 0.10. So P(pass) = 0.30 + 0.10 = 0.40. Then P(studied given passed) = 0.30 / 0.40 = 0.75.

distractor_logic.A: Student reports the joint probability P(study and pass) = 0.30 without dividing by the total pass probability.
distractor_logic.B: Student reports the reversed conditional P(pass given study) = 0.60 instead of P(study given pass).
distractor_logic.C: Student reports the prior probability of studying (0.50), ignoring that the student passed.

strategy_hints.hint_text: Find the chance of passing along each path (studied or not), add them for the total pass rate, then put the studied-and-passed path over that total.
Sketch a tree: first branch is study or not, second branch is pass or not. Multiply along the passing branches before combining.

author: jd-oviedo

objective_text: Probability, Sample Spaces, and Set Foundations
skills_targeted: computing a conditional probability whose condition is a derived attribute total
identifying which group the condition selects when it is not the larger category
question_text: Of 80 people surveyed, 50 prefer tea and 30 prefer coffee. Among the tea drinkers, 30 take sugar. Among the coffee drinkers, 12 take sugar. Given that a person takes sugar, what is the probability that the person prefers coffee?
explanation: The condition is 'takes sugar'. The sugar takers total 30 + 12 = 42. Of those, 12 prefer coffee, so P(coffee given sugar) = 12/42 = 2/7.

distractor_logic.A: Student divides the coffee-and-sugar count by all 80 people, getting 12/80 = 3/20, instead of by the sugar takers.
distractor_logic.B: Student reverses the condition and computes P(sugar given coffee) = 12/30 = 2/5.
distractor_logic.C: Correct: there are 42 sugar takers, of whom 12 prefer coffee, so 12/42 = 2/7.
distractor_logic.D: Student reports the probability of preferring coffee overall (30/80 = 3/8), ignoring the sugar condition.

strategy_hints.hint_text: The condition is 'takes sugar', so build that group by adding the sugar takers from both the tea and coffee sides.
P(coffee given sugar) is not P(sugar given coffee). Watch for the answer that divides by the 30 coffee drinkers.

author: jd-oviedo
